summary: When UCLA invited me to become chancellor, it was a pivotal moment in my career. I arrived with a vision to strengthen academic quality and faced many challenges like budget battles and campus autonomy. I was deeply invested in enhancing the university's library and supporting the arts, which led to the creation of the UCLA Sculpture Garden bearing my name. My time at UCLA was intense but rewarding, especially as I worked closely with faculty, alumni, and community members like the Chandlers.

Before coming to UCLA, I was at the University of Kansas, where I had built a strong foundation in university leadership. It was there that I received the unexpected invitation from Clark Kerr to consider the chancellorship at UCLA. Reflecting on my time at Kansas, I believe I helped transform the institution by bringing energy and a commitment to excellence, which prepared me for the challenges ahead in California.

Clark Kerr played a crucial role in my career by inviting me to consider the chancellorship at UCLA while I was at the University of Kansas. Throughout my tenure, I maintained a working relationship with Kerr and other university officials, navigating the complexities of authority and governance. His involvement was significant in shaping the leadership transition and the broader University of California system's direction during that period.

entity: University of California Board of Regents
summary: My meeting with the University of California Board of Regents was a defining moment when discussing my appointment as UCLA chancellor. They were receptive to my vision, especially regarding academic quality and the importance of the library as a symbol of distinction. Engaging with the regents helped solidify my plans for the university and underscored the administrative challenges ahead.

The Reagan administration brought political changes that significantly affected the university environment during my time at UCLA. These shifts contributed to the intensity and demands of my role, which eventually led to my decision to step down as chancellor. Navigating the university through this period required careful attention to the evolving political landscape and its impact on higher education.

After stepping down as UCLA chancellor, I accepted the opportunity to chair the board of the Times Mirror Company. This role marked a transition from academic administration to leadership in the media industry. It was a new challenge that came at a time when I was seeking a change following the demanding years at UCLA.

The UCLA Sculpture Garden is a personal testament to my commitment to the arts during my chancellorship. I supported modern sculpture and ethnic arts, believing that programs combining brilliance and enthusiasm enriched the university community. Having the garden bear my name reflects the passion I had for integrating art and scholarship at UCLA.

When I first visited UCLA, I was warmly received by the Chandler family, who were important community members. Their support, along with that of alumni and faculty, helped me understand the campus culture and expectations for the chancellor's role. The Chandlers played a key part in welcoming me and fostering connections between the university and the broader community.
